name: Hu, Yu Hen
recent_1: Sun, Y.-H., Ke, J., Fernandes, J., Chen, J., Jiang, H., & Hu, Y. H. (2025). EasyVis: a real-time 3D visualization software system for laparoscopic surgery box trainer. Updates in Surgery, 1-16. DOI: 10.1007/s13304-025-02153-w
recent_1_synopsis: This paper presents EasyVis, a real-time stereo 3D visualization system that integrates micro-cameras and light sources into laparoscopic surgical ports to provide intra-abdominal stereo vision at a chosen viewpoint. The system is validated in a laparoscopic training-box environment. It targets improved depth perception and surgical-training efficiency by giving trainees a reconstructed 3D view rather than a flat monoscopic image.
recent_2: Chen, J., Sun, Y.-H., Pickett, K. A., King, B., Hu, Y. H., & Jiang, H. (2025). A Wearable Gait Monitoring System for 17 Gait Parameters Based on Computer Vision. IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement, 74, art. 5027114, 1-14. DOI: 10.1109/TIM.2025.3557814
recent_2_synopsis: This study introduces a shoe-mounted system that uses a stereo camera tracking a marker on the opposite shoe to recover spatial parameters and a heel-mounted force-sensitive resistor to recover temporal parameters, jointly measuring 17 gait parameters. Reported accuracy exceeds 93.6% across all parameters. The work supports low-cost continuous gait analysis outside the laboratory for clinical and rehabilitation monitoring.
recent_3: Sun, Y.-H., Shen, G., Chen, J., Fernandes, J., Shada, A. L., Heise, C. P., Jiang, H., & Hu, Y. H. (2025). EasyVis2: A real-time multi-view 3D visualization system for laparoscopic surgery training enhanced by a deep neural network YOLOv8-pose. (arXiv:2412.16742) [no Crossref-resolvable DOI located]
recent_3_synopsis: This work extends EasyVis to multi-view 3D reconstruction, using a YOLOv8-Pose deep network (fine-tuned on a custom surgical-tool dataset) to estimate instrument pose in each camera view and fuse the views into a 3D surface model. It reports improved 3D reconstruction accuracy and reduced computation time versus the prior system, with demonstrations on real animal tissue. It illustrates the deep-learning/computer-vision direction of his recent surgical-training work.
seminal_1: Li, D., Wong, K. D., Hu, Y. H., & Sayeed, A. M. (2002). Detection, classification, and tracking of targets. IEEE Signal Processing Magazine, 19(2), 17-29. DOI: 10.1109/79.985674
seminal_1_synopsis: This article laid out an influential framework for collaborative signal processing in distributed/wireless sensor networks, covering event detection, target classification, and location estimation/prediction of moving targets. With roughly 1,600 citations (Google Scholar, approximate, 2026-06-17), it is his most-cited work. It is foundational because it became a standard reference defining the architecture and problem decomposition for sensor-network target tracking, shaping a decade of follow-on research.
seminal_2: Sheng, X., & Hu, Y. H. (2005). Maximum likelihood multiple-source localization using acoustic energy measurements with wireless sensor networks. IEEE Transactions on Signal Processing, 53(1), 44-53. DOI: 10.1109/TSP.2004.838930
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper introduced a maximum-likelihood method for localizing multiple acoustic sources from energy measurements across an ad hoc wireless sensor network, outperforming prior energy-based approaches and adding multi-source capability. With roughly 950 citations (Google Scholar, approximate, 2026-06-17), it is a widely cited methodological cornerstone for energy-based acoustic source localization in sensor networks.
expertise_overview: Work sits at the intersection of statistical signal processing, machine learning, and computer vision, with deep roots in algorithm design and hardware-efficient implementation, including CORDIC-based and parallel VLSI architectures for digital signal processing. A major thread is distributed and wireless sensor networks, contributing widely used methods for acoustic source localization and for detection, classification, and tracking of targets. A long-standing biomedical signal-processing line applies neural networks and adaptive filtering to ECG analysis, QRS detection, and patient-adaptable beat classification. More recent methods center on deep learning and computer vision applied to human-centered and clinical problems: markerless pose estimation, multi-view 3D reconstruction for laparoscopic surgery, gait and motion monitoring, and video-based ergonomic exposure assessment. Across domains the approach emphasizes probabilistic and machine-learning estimation from sensor and image data, real-time and computationally efficient processing, and translation into practical instrumentation, with application domains spanning sensor networks, biomedical and surgical systems, ergonomics, and image/video analytics.
notes: High-confidence identification. Yu Hen Hu, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, University of Wisconsin-Madison (IEEE Fellow); identity confirmed via Google Scholar (user=5CAjat0AAAAJ, verified wisc.edu email), the UW-Madison ECE faculty directory, and ResearchGate, and anchored by the provided hf_papers, which are his applied computer-vision/ergonomics collaborations (with Radwin, Zinn, Mutlu). His core seminal work is in signal processing / wireless sensor networks, consistent with his career profile. Independently verified via the Crossref API on 2026-06-17 (HTTP 200, matching titles): 10.1007/s13304-025-02153-w, 10.1109/TIM.2025.3557814, 10.1109/79.985674, 10.1109/TSP.2004.838930. UNVERIFIABLE DOI FLAG: recent_3 (EasyVis2) has no Crossref-resolvable journal DOI; only an arXiv preprint (arXiv:2412.16742) was located, so it is included WITHOUT a DOI and not fabricated. Seminal citation counts (~1,600 and ~950) are approximate, from Google Scholar as of 2026-06-17 (profile totals ~16,968 citations, h-index 52).
